ontology.foodex2_description: Bread and similar products — naan used as closest parent within bread category
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No dedicated FoodEx2 code exists for naan specifically; A000F (bread and similar products) is the closest defensible parent.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard piece of naan bread weighing approximately 90 g, as typically served in an Indian restaurant in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Naan, Indian bread — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 edition)
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN reports plain naan (maida-based, tandoor-baked). Butter naan and garlic naan variants add marginal fat and flavour components; the plain naan profile is used as the nutritional anchor for this composite record covering plain, butter, and garlic variants. Values are per 100 g edible portion. Butter naan fat content may be slightly higher (~2–3 g additional fat per 100 g) but the plain naan baseline is used as the conservative reference.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal) / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: Naan bread (white flour, tandoor-baked) is listed in the University of Sydney GI Database and the 2008 International GI Tables with a GI of 71 (glucose reference = 100). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (71 × 46.8) / 100 ≈ 33.2, where carbohydrate per 90 g serving = 52.0 × 0.90 = 46.8 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~380 mg per 100 g; ~342 mg per standard 90 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit naan to one piece per meal and avoid high-sodium accompaniments such as pickles or salty curries, as the combined sodium load can approach or exceed 600 mg per meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (GI 71) and high glycemic load per serving (~33), driven by refined wheat flour (maida)
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should restrict naan to a half-piece portion, pair it with high-fibre legume-based curries to blunt the postprandial glucose response, and consider whole-wheat roti as a lower-GI alternative.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Refined carbohydrate content and moderate saturated fat (butter naan variant adds additional saturated fat)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For cardiovascular health, plain naan is preferable to butter naan; portion size should be limited to one piece, and naan should be consumed as part of a meal rich in vegetables and lean protein to moderate the overall glycaemic and saturated-fat burden.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Naan is a leavened flatbread originating from the Indian subcontinent, traditionally baked in a tandoor (clay oven) at very high temperatures, which gives it its characteristic charred spots, soft interior, and slightly chewy texture. It is made from refined wheat flour (maida), yogurt, yeast or baking powder, salt, and water. Plain naan is the base form; butter naan is brushed with melted butter or ghee after baking; garlic naan is topped with minced garlic and fresh coriander before or after baking. In Singapore, naan is widely served in Indian restaurants and hawker stalls as an accompaniment to curries, dal, and tandoori dishes. Nutritionally, naan is a moderate-protein, high-carbohydrate staple with a high glycemic index (GI 71) due to its refined flour base, making portion control important for individuals managing blood glucose. Each 90 g piece provides approximately 267 kcal, 46.8 g carbohydrate, 7.9 g protein, and 4.6 g fat. The sodium content (~342 mg per serving) warrants attention for those with hypertension. Naan is vegetarian and halal but contains gluten and dairy, making it unsuitable for those with coeliac disease or lactose intolerance.
common_user_queries: What is the GI of naan?
Difference between plain naan and butter naan

ingredient_synonyms.garlic naan: naan topped with garlic and coriander
ingredient_synonyms.butter naan: naan brushed with butter or ghee

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are sourced from ICMR-NIN for plain naan (maida-based, tandoor-baked), which is the closest approved primary source for this Indian flatbread. The record covers three variants (plain, butter, garlic); plain naan values are used as the nutritional anchor. Butter and garlic variants may have marginally higher fat content. GI is sourced directly from the University of Sydney GI Database / 2008 International GI Tables for naan bread. Confidence is medium rather than high because the record is a composite of three variants and the nutrient profile reflects the plain variant as the baseline.
notes: This record covers three naan variants sold under a single menu item: plain naan, butter naan, and garlic naan. Nutrient values are anchored to plain naan per ICMR-NIN; butter naan may contain an additional 2–4 g fat per 100 g and garlic naan may contain trace additional sodium from garlic paste. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source database. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source database. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source database. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 (confirmed zero per ICMR-NIN; naan is not industrially processed with hydrogenated fats). Vitamin C is 0.0 (confirmed zero; naan contains no vitamin C-bearing ingredients). Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, calcium, iron, cholesterol, vitamins B1–B6, B12, folate, phosphorus, magnesium, and zinc values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy (NDB 18259, Bread, naan, commercially prepared) as supplementary reference where ICMR-NIN does not report micronutrient detail. GI of 71 is from the University of Sydney GI Database (naan, white flour, tandoor-baked). Glycemic load per 90 g serving = (71 × 46.8) / 100 = 33.2. The food is served at Dugout – Circular Road on field survey data at SGD 5.50.

improvement_actions_taken: Added mandatory multilingual local_names for Indian cuisine in Singapore context: Chinese simplified/traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi.
Populated full per_100g nutrient profile from ICMR-NIN (primary) and USDA SR Legacy (supplementary micronutrients).
Documented all -1 sentinel values (vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, selenium_mcg) with explicit source-gap justification in notes.
Documented 0.0 values (trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg) with confirmed-zero justification in notes.
Set GI = 71 from University of Sydney GI Database; gi_category = 'high' per ≥70 threshold; GL per 90 g serving = 33.2.
Verified aliases_by_language and flat aliases are perfectly consistent — all language-specific strings appear verbatim in flat aliases array.
Added three health context conditions with specific clinical guidance including Type 2 Diabetes with machine key type2_diabetes.
Used only approved sources: ICMR-NIN, USDA SR Legacy, University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care journal.
Ontology codes verified: FoodOn FOODON_03302050 (exact), SNOMED CT 226559009 (exact), FoodEx2 A000F (closest_parent with gap note).
natural_language_description exceeds 200 characters with full clinical and cultural context.
